utterances: I had exactly the same experience, Corey.
This is Phil Manning.
Well a lot of - a lot of people did.
We're talking about science books this hour on TALK OF THE NATION: SCIENCE FRIDAY from NPR News.
Phil, do you think there are a lot of books like that out?
Maybe Stephen Walfrom's(ph) book, you know, about the huge, huge…
The Road to Reality.
…yeah, I mean this huge book that was very hard to get through.
It was almost impossible to get through.
First of all, it starts - I think it's over 800 pages long.
And the first 400 pages are devoted to giving you the mathematics that you will need to understand the next 400 pages.
And I'm talking about Vector calculus and things like this.
It's not - it actually makes "A Brief History of Time" look pretty readable.
And it's very difficult.

What I've actually found in talking with a lot of people - and we have a big science book club down here - is that the people that know - that are more interested in science but don't, aren't, don't have technical backgrounds, that's the kind of books they're interested in, the brief history of science.
They want to read that kind of book.
They want to read about multiple universes and brains and string theory and things of that nature that those of us that have technical backgrounds are kind of leery of.
Do you…
So it's interesting to see.
Well, just on the periphery, do you get into reviewing science fiction books at all that you might suggest?
Or Corey, any one come to mind?
Or Heather, do you have any - I guess that would be in the fiction section.
So he's probably not talking anyway, but he's writing communication.

Well, I have to say, when Heather was talking about books for children, I mean I think, yeah, the science books - when I think back to the books that influenced me most growing up, I remember the fiction books that dealt with science at least as much as I do in the actual science books.

A book called "The Enormous Egg" by a writer named Oliver Butterworth, which is written, I think late ‘60s or maybe very early ‘70s, and it's - it deals with remarkably sophisticated ideas about evolution and the connection between birds and dinosaurs, but does it in the form of a complete fantasy story of a chicken that lays a dinosaur egg.
And a child raises it and learns all this, you know, learns about the connections between birds and dinosaurs.
It's a beautiful book.
Any classic books on your list for kids?
Are any kids books on that top 25?
You know, that word great is such a loaded word that, you know, it steered us away from children's books.

But honestly, you know, the problem of writing a popular book for children is even more acute than writing, you know, a persuasive book for adults, which I think is probably actually why they're not on the list in most of those.
Most of those books tend to be somewhat ephemeral.
I remember my Danny Dunn book and his flying machine.
How about some of the George Emoph(ph) books.
Hm-hmm.
Yeah.
Actually, I mean there is one book actually that I think might be appropriate for children, which is Roy Chapman Andrews, who is one of the great dinosaur hunters.
And he's one of the 10 or 20 or 100 people who could kind of be the model for Indiana Jones.
But he wrote these wonderful swashbuckling adventures of driving in Mongolia looking for dinosaur bones.
And they're - I mean, they're beautiful, exciting books.
And actually, you know, I read them as a kid and I still remember them vividly.
Yeah.
And they're still relevant today.
I want to thank all of you for taking time to talk with us.
Time goes by so fast.
As I say, we'll have these books on the list.
Charles will have them up by the end of the day, in case you didn't get them down and you want to re-review them.
Phil Manning is science writer in Chapel Hill, North Carolina and book reviewer for the News & Observer in Raleigh, the San Francisco Chronicle, and the Atlanta Journal-Constitution.
Busy guy.
Thanks for taking time for us today, Phil.
Thanks for having me.
Enjoy.
Heather Malcomson - you're welcome - is editor of Science Books and Films, a publication of the AAAS in Washington.
And Corey Powell is executive editor at Discover magazine here in New York.

When we come back, we're going to talk about viewing the night sky.

There's going to be a full eclipse, a total eclipse of the moon coming up this winter.
Joe Rao is going to be here, back with us, to talk about what to see in the night sky.
Don't go away.
We'll be right back, also with some advice on how to buy your first telescope.
It could be daunting, you know.
So stay with us.
We'll be right back after this break.
Here's an effort to make you know what it feels like to walk in the shoes of a refugee.
More than 65 million people around the world have been forced to leave their homes.
The U.N.
says they're fleeing violence, persecution, disasters.
Doctors Without Borders hopes to show Americans what that life is like.
They have a traveling exhibit they're taking to cities across the Northeast.
NPR's Nurith Aizenman joined the tour when it stopped in Washington, D.C.
Hi, guys.
How are you?
Our guide is Dr. Ahmed Abdalrazag, who normally works in a refugee camp in Tunisia.
And he tells our little group to imagine that war has just broken out.
We're going to have to leave our homes, our town.
Then he takes us to this wall hung with plastic cards listing our most essential possessions.
And he makes a startling announcement.
So what will I ask you guys, in 30 seconds, pick only five items.
In other words, we have 30 seconds to go through the cards and pick just five things to take with us.
Abdalrazag holds up a stopwatch.
OK.
In 30 seconds, go.
There's a mad scramble for the cards.
Five items only.
Rita Kietzer - she's a 55-year-old homemaker - drove two hours from Virginia just for this tour.
She looks preoccupied.
Was it hard?
Yeah, a lot of choices.
Ten seconds.
I had to really look it over.
Two, one - you're out of time.
Abdalrazag inspects our picks.
What did you get here?
Cash, blankets, medicine.
He notices no one brought shoes - possible mistake.
During the 1991 Persian Gulf War when Abdalrazag was a kid, Saddam Hussein's government started bombing his town in southern Iraq.
He and his family had to run.
So we walked and we walked.
We walked for 10 days - 10 days non-stop.
I remember I had flip-flops on my feet.
On the 10th day, I had nothing on my feet.
No matter what you bring, he says, you're going to lose it fast - to pay off smugglers or you'll get robbed.
To illustrate this, Abdalrazag holds up a white box.
Everyone has to throw in one of their five items.
(SOUNDBITE OF CARDS FALLING INTO BOX)
I'm seeing passports, motorcycle, cellphone.
OK.
Let's move to our next station.
We're led to one of those inflatable boats.
